mcqs.question: What is the correct syntax to declare a pure virtual function in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Pure virtual functions are declared using the = 0 syntax after the function signature. This makes the function abstract with no implementation in the base class.

mcqs.question: Which of the following statements is TRUE about abstract classes?

options.text: Abstract classes can be instantiated directly
Abstract classes must contain at least one pure virtual function
Abstract classes cannot have regular member functions

mcqs.correctAnswer: Abstract classes must contain at least one pure virtual function
mcqs.explanation: An abstract class is defined as a class that contains at least one pure virtual function. It cannot be instantiated directly.

mcqs.question: What happens if a derived class does not override ALL pure virtual functions?

options.text: The program runs with undefined behavior
The derived class also becomes abstract

mcqs.correctAnswer: The derived class also becomes abstract
mcqs.explanation: If a derived class fails to implement all pure virtual functions from its base class, it remains abstract and cannot be instantiated.

mcqs.question: Which keyword is used to prevent instantiation of a class in C++?
mcqs.explanation: C++ does not have a dedicated 'abstract' keyword. Classes become abstract by containing at least one pure virtual function (= 0).

mcqs.question: Can a class have both pure virtual functions and regular functions with implementations?

options.text: Only if all functions are virtual

mcqs.explanation: Abstract classes can have both pure virtual functions (which derived classes must implement) and regular member functions with implementations.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of making a destructor virtual in an abstract class?

options.text: To enable runtime polymorphism for destructors

mcqs.correctAnswer: To enable runtime polymorphism for destructors
mcqs.explanation: Virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when objects are deleted through base class pointers, calling derived class destructors first.

mcqs.question: Which design pattern commonly uses pure virtual functions?
mcqs.explanation: Factory Pattern uses abstract base classes with pure virtual functions to create objects without specifying their exact classes.

mcqs.question: What is an interface in C++ equivalent to?

options.text: A class with all public members
An abstract class with only pure virtual functions
A class with private members only

mcqs.correctAnswer: An abstract class with only pure virtual functions
mcqs.explanation: In C++, interfaces are typically implemented as abstract classes containing only pure virtual functions that define the contract.

mcqs.question: Can constructors be declared as virtual functions?
mcqs.explanation: Constructors cannot be virtual in C++. The compiler handles constructor calls at compile time based on the object type being created.

mcqs.question: What is the vtable in C++?

options.text: A virtual table for runtime type identification
A lookup table for virtual function dispatch

mcqs.correctAnswer: A lookup table for virtual function dispatch
mcqs.explanation: A vtable (virtual table) is a mechanism used by C++ compilers to support dynamic dispatch of virtual function calls at runtime.

mcqs.question: When is the constructor of an abstract class called?

options.text: When creating objects of derived classes

mcqs.correctAnswer: When creating objects of derived classes
mcqs.explanation: When a derived class object is created, the base class constructor is called to initialize the base portion of the object.

mcqs.question: What is object slicing?

options.text: Copying only part of an object when assigned to base class object
Deleting a derived class object through base pointer

mcqs.correctAnswer: Copying only part of an object when assigned to base class object
mcqs.explanation: Object slicing occurs when a derived class object is assigned to a base class object, losing the derived portion. This can be prevented using pointers or references.

mcqs.question: Which of the following CANNOT be done with an abstract class?

options.text: Create pointers or references to it

mcqs.explanation: The primary restriction on abstract classes is that they cannot be instantiated directly. They can still be used as base classes and for creating pointers/references.

mcqs.question: What is late binding in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Late binding (or dynamic binding) occurs when function calls are resolved at runtime, enabled by virtual functions and the vtable mechanism.

mcqs.question: Can a pure virtual function have a default implementation?

options.text: Yes, but it serves no purpose
Yes, derived classes can call it using ClassName::functionName()

mcqs.correctAnswer: Yes, derived classes can call it using ClassName::functionName()
mcqs.explanation: Pure virtual functions can have implementations. Derived classes can explicitly call the base class implementation using the scope resolution operator.

mcqs.question: What is the diamond problem in C++?

options.text: Ambiguity in multiple inheritance with common base

mcqs.correctAnswer: Ambiguity in multiple inheritance with common base
mcqs.explanation: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base, creating ambiguity in member access. Virtual inheritance solves this.

mcqs.question: Which access specifier is typically used for pure virtual functions in interfaces?
mcqs.explanation: In interface design, pure virtual functions are typically declared as public to define the contract that derived classes must implement for external use.

mcqs.question: What is runtime polymorphism achieved through?
mcqs.explanation: Runtime polymorphism in C++ is achieved through virtual functions, allowing the appropriate function to be called based on the actual object type at runtime.

mcqs.question: How many pure virtual functions must a class contain to be abstract?

options.text: All functions must be pure virtual

mcqs.explanation: A class becomes abstract when it contains at least one pure virtual function (declared with = 0 syntax).

mcqs.question: What is the output of deleting a derived object through a base pointer without virtual destructor?
mcqs.explanation: Without a virtual destructor, deleting through a base pointer leads to undefined behavior - typically only the base destructor is called, causing resource leaks.
